frameworks.name: neural decoding
frameworks.one_line: inferring decoding weights of brain areas from population activity

frameworks.name: choice probability / neuron-behaviour correlation
frameworks.one_line: extended to jointly account for inactivation effects

frameworks.name: interneuronal noise correlations
frameworks.one_line: correlation structure shapes which decoder is consistent

core_question: Can decoding weights across multiple correlated neural populations be inferred without precise knowledge of the correlation structure?
key_assumptions: choice-related activity and inactivation effects share a common decoding model
noise correlations exist between populations but need not be measured precisely
behaviour reflects a linear readout of population responses
manipulations: combining choice-correlation data with inactivation outcomes
varying assumed structure of correlated noise across scenarios
applying inference to two different cortical areas during heading discrimination
key_findings: decoding weights can be inferred without measuring the full correlation matrix
two opposing decoding schemes are both consistent with the macaque data
which scheme holds depends on the nature of correlated noise
interpretations: choice-only theories cannot reconcile correlation and causal results without additional constraints
combining correlation and perturbation data yields testable predictions distinguishing decoding schemes
limitations_noted: scheme identification still requires future experiments to disambiguate the correlated-noise scenario
